sentences.text: No, I won't bring in Walsh in it.
It's OK.
My name is Mike Walsh.
M-I-K-E W-A-L-S-H.
No, my name was Mordechai Waks at home, and my parents' name.
But for some reason, in Germany, it was changed.
And I never really changed it back.
But I go by both names.
I was born in the Ukraine, in a small village called Male Werbcze.
And I was born in Purim, actually-- April 1, 1930.
M-A-L-E.
The second word is V-E-R-B-C-Z-E.
My name was-- my father's name was Shaul Waks.
I think he was born in the same place, in the village.
Because the family has lived for many years in Male Werbcze.
I couldn't tell you how many generations.
But I know that they were established many years and well-known in the area.
My father was born in 1887.
That's what, approximately, I remember.
And he said that he was born in the area.
No, my father was killed in 1942 after they took him from the ghetto with my-- my sister.
My father was taken from the ghetto with my sister to [?
Kochivle, ?] from the Stepan ghetto.
And there they were doing the extermination of the Jews.
My second brother, Yakov, was with them.
But he escaped.
And my sister had a chance to escape while they were go-- taking them for the killing, but she didn't want to leave my father alone.
So she went along with my father.
There were some people that survived after the killing.
There were a couple of people that were injured.
And also, my brother, the one that escaped, it was only about 10 minutes before they arrived to the place where they did the extermination.
And he escaped into the woods.
So he knows that they took him there, with my sister.
My father was a hazzan.
And he was going from town to town on high holidays to do the prayers.
And we had a little farm in the village-- a cow, a horse.
And this is what he did.
A hazzan and the farm.
Yeah.
No, just the farm was just for our own use.
And we were still buying from some neighbors.
Before he-- when he was with his parents, they had an inn in the village.
And they also had a tar factory, which they called Waks.
And I presume that maybe the name they took on years ago that my grandfather had operated this factory for tar, which they called Waks.
Maybe the name came from there.
I don't know.
My father's father name was Shabtai.
And my grandmother's name was Yona-- [?
Tibul ?] in Yiddish.
No, I don't remember my father's grandparents.
I remember my mother's grandparents.
My grandfather's name from my mother's side was Yeshayahu.
And my grandmother's name was Chana.
Their family name was Taks, T-A-K-S.
I couldn't tell you.
I don't remember what my grandfather did.
He seemed to be very-- they seemed to be very old.
They used to come to visit then, at the time.
And when I knew them, they were already, like, what you call old and retired.
Or he was always going to shul.
But that's all I know.
My mother's name was Chaya.
No.
When they took us to the ghetto, I was left in the village.
And I took care of a child and helped a farmer, a neighbor.
And at the same time, they left me there.
And at the same time, I was actually bringing the food to the village.
We had some clothing, and some other furniture, and so on.
And my brother used to sell it to the people and then get some money and buy food.
And he also worked.
Yitshak didn't go to the ghetto either.
Because I had an uncle that had a factory of, like, a paint.
What do you call it-- sid-- what do-- you know, like to paint, such white stuff that they used to manufacture.
And the Germans kept him and my uncle in the village, in this factory.
And he used to earn a little bit of money.
And he used to buy food.
And I used to be dressed as a Ukrainian kid, and jump into the ghetto through the fence, and bring food for my parents.
And we had to still be careful not to bring any meats or anything, because they were still kosher in the ghetto.
Now you asked me about my mother.
The day-- in 1942, in August '42, when they took us all to the [?
Kochivle ?] killing grounds, that night, somehow I was lonesome, and I came to visit my parents.
I was 12 years old.
And that night exactly that I stayed with my parents, they started to gather us to take us to the killing.
We were in a room in the house where we stayed, a little house.
There were about eight families.
Each one had a room, or in a room maybe two families.
I was with my mother, my sister, and my father.
My other brothers, three of them, were in the labor camp.
And my younger second brother, Yakov, was in another section in the ghetto.
Around 2 o'clock in the morning, they started to gather people.
We hid in a-- in the attic.
They put-- took all the people that they found.
And around 2:30, 3 o'clock, probably close to 4 o'clock in the morning, they started to look for us-- the Germans and the Ukrainian Nazis.
So we opened the window.
And we started to jump.
And my mother asked me to jump first.
And then she jumped after me.
And we just heard a lot of shooting in the room.
And they-- my father and my sister couldn't escape anymore.
The Germans came with the Ukrainian Nazis and picked them up.
And my mother and I escaped nearby to a Ukrainian barn, a barn a Ukrainian farmer had.
And we stayed there until daylight.
And at daylight, came in the farmer and saw us, and chased us out.
So we were on our way to go to our village.
As we were going to the village, we just didn't remember.
I used to go every week.
But I got all upset.
And I didn't remember the way back to the village, Male Werbcze.
So we went into a farmer, not far from the Jewish cemetery in Stepan, and asked the way to go to Werbcze.
And this farmer came up to us and says, oh, you are Jews.
You killed Christ.
You are communists.
And you should die too-- took my mother and me by the shoulders.
He didn't have a weapon or anything.
And he just was leading us to the police station, where they were gathering probably the ones that escaped, and then taking them back for the extermination.
We were walking.
As we walked, we-- I recognized the road that goes to the village, Male Werbcze.
And I said in Yiddish to my mother that this is where we go to Male Werbcze.
My mother started to beg the Ukrainian, please, let us go.
Let us go.
He wouldn't.
So she says, let the kid go and you can go with him.
And we have buried some silver or whatever, some money near our house.
And he will give you everything.
And she told me where it was.
He says, oh, no.
No way.
In that case, my mother said, try to escape, to me.
Since I was really a village kid, I acted very fast.
I fell down to the ground and started to run.
The Ukrainian bandit didn't run after me.
He took my mother.
And I never saw her again.
And I escaped into the forest.
And then at night, I went to a close friend that I went to school, the mayor's son, the mayor of the village son.
His name was Ivan.
And he took care of me.
And the father hid me.
The next day, the mayor's-- the mayor went to the city of Stepan, to the town.
And he wanted to see my mother.
So he went down to the police station.
And he saw my mother.
He visited her, and talked to her, and told her in secret that I escaped and he will take care of me.
And my mother said to send regards and she thanked him very much.
And she said she hoped that I would stay alive for her sake, and I should take care of myself.
August 1942.
Yeah, in a few days, yes.
We were five brothers and one sister.
The eldest brother-- his name was Shabtai.
He was born, from what I know, about 1914, Shabtai.
And then my sister-- next was sister-- 1916.
Her name was Sonia.
And the next, my brother Yitshak-- he was born 1918.
And then I had a brother Chaim.
He was born in 1926.
Yakov was born in 1928.
And then myself-- I was born in 1930.
No, there were no others.
Yeah, I was the youngest, yes.
Yes.
Well, after the extermination, my brother Yakov escaped, like I said before, from the-- practically near the killing.
He escaped into the village.
And he survived then.
Then my other two brothers, Chaim and Shabtai, were in a labor camp in a town of Kostopol, which was about 30 kilometers west from Stepan.
These were all young Jewish men from the age of 16 and up were kept there in labor camps.
During the night, when the extermination started in Kostopol of the labor camp, they all broke the barriers of the gates and some of the walls. 80% or 90% of them were all killed.
But my brother Shabtai and Chaim escaped into the village.
Into the village of Male Werbcze, where we came from, because they knew there the people would help us out.
So actually, all five of us survived for that time.
We were all into hiding with our neighbors and friends in the village of Male Werbcze and the area.
And nobody touched us.
The people were really, I would say, good people.
The only surviving brother I have now is Yitshak, who lives in Israel with his family.
My brothers were killed.
One, Shabtai, was killed in 19-- the end of 1942, beg-- I think it was February-- sorry-- 1943.
He was caught by some of the Ukrainian bandits and taken to the Germans.
And he was killed.
And another brother, Chaim, who was in the military, like, police force to take care of all the Nazis-- Ukrainian Nazis-- in the Soviet police-- he was killed in October 1944.
And Yakov was also in the same police.
He was killed by the Ukrainian Nazis in 1945-- sorry, '47, 1947, after the war.
Well, the Ukraine-- since he was in the police and he was after getting-- after those Nazis, they knew where he was staying.
And they attacked the unit that he was with.
And he was killed with some other police forces.
Well, we lived in a village.
There was no address really, like, in there, no number.
There was just a small village.
The meaning of 'Male' means small. 'Werbcze,' which means-- just to explain-- 'werbcze' means--
--willow trees.
And they did have a lot of willows in there.
And we lived in a house.
And all I know is we had a big sign there, [? 'Shavil ?] Waks,' which if somebody was looking, he could see the name on the house.
Actually, in Ukrainian, it was [? 'Shavil.' ?] It  was 'Shaul' pronounced.
The house was made of wood.
There was no foundation.
It was sitting on the ground foundation with some-- like, made of logs.
And the roof was made of straw, [LAUGHS] like in a story town.
And I remember when I was five years old, there was a fire.
Because I guess the house was heated with wood and a spark went on the roof.
And since the roof was made of straw, the fire caught very fast.
And we lost the house in-- in minutes.
And a neighbor, a Ukrainian neighbor came and took me in.
That I remember.
Like I say, it was in 1935.
And then we were taken care of by a Ukrainian neighbor, until my father got insurance.
I was surprised, in those days, that they had insurance.
But I do remember that he collected insurance.
And we bought another house from another neighbor.
I even remember his name.
The name was Makar.
And he sold us the house with a big, big stall in it.
And then we lived until we were taken away, in this house, with a farm.
Just about a block away, yes.
It's the family name, yeah.
Unfortunately not.
I do remember one name.
It was across the road.
He took care of us.
Nichipor was his name.
Patrikei-- his last name.
And the lady that took me in-- she was actually the midwife that delivered me.
And they called her, in Ukrainian or whatever-- Yiddish, [YIDDISH].
She was the one that delivered me when I was born.
So I was in her place.
It had only a kitchen and-- I don't remember much about that house.
But I do remember it was just like one room, the whole house.
And the new house had the same thing.
It had one bedroom, and the kitchen, and a living room.
Yes, my family was all Orthodox.
And my father, like I said, was a hazzan, so.
At home, we spoke Yiddish.
And naturally, since we lived in a Ukrainian village, we spoke Ukrainian as well.
I went, in Male Werbcze, to a public school.
And in the summer, we used to go to a cheder, where I started at the age of three.
And since we were very few families in the village of Male Werbcze, there was another village called [?
Vilica ?] Werbcze, which is the big village of Werbcze.
And there they had more Jewish families.
And they had a cheder there.
And my father used to teach us in our own house with some other neighbors' kids.
It's hard to say.
My mother seemed to be very good natured-- my father too, getting along very well with the neighbors.

And from what I saw, the way the neighbors took care of us and helped us during the war to feed us, and helped us to-- to hide us from the Germans and the Nazis, I find it was really a legacy, which was left that the people really cared for us and because we cared for our neighbors.
It's hard to say.
Because their life was a very hard life.
You worked day and night.
My mother, with six kids, had to do all the housework, and wash the laundry in the river, and get the water from the well across in the village.
So-- but we had a lot of entertainment with some of the Jewish neighbors, with the neighbors from the villages, my grandparents that lived in the next village, called [?
Korist. ?] And also, a lot of the neighbors used to come in and gather in our house.
And I remember staying up till late at night.
They were always talking, and discussing politics, and everything else, like today.
Well, the village itself, I would say, was maybe about 120, 130 families.
And then surrounding, there were big farmers.
And there were, around there, about maybe another 30, 40 home-- families in there.
Well, there were only-- in our village, there was my aunt and uncle.
My mother's sister got married.
This I remember.
Her name was Beila.
And she was married to a man by the name of Moishe, who was born in the village.
And his parents lived not far from us.
Their name was Golda and Itshak, his parents.
And they were married.
And they lived in our village of Male Werbcze.
And they had two children.
There was another lady, a widow, that we used to help out.
She lived also in that village.
She was all alone.
I think she was a widow.
But I never saw any kids.
Maybe she was never married.
I don't know.
Two-- three Jewish families in our village, that's all.
But all the other villages nearby had much more Yiddish-- Jewish-- many more Jewish families.
The nearest village was [?
Korist, ?] where my grandparents lived.
And there were probably about 20 families in there.
That would be about three to four kilometers.
And another village, about six kilometers, which was [?
Krichilsk. ?] And they had, I would say, about 30 families-- a big village.
And [?
Vilica ?] Werbcze, the next village, had also about 20 families.
So during the holidays, we used to get together.
Or Shabbos, they used to come to visit us, or we would visit them.
Yes.
Yeah, yeah.
We didn't have any Jewish friends.
So we were playing always with the Ukrainian friends.
Yes.
There was a girl that went to school with me.
Her name was Katya.
And one girl was Nastia Patrikei.
And my friend Ivan Solovei-- that's the mayor's son.
And I had some other friends that I don't remember off hand now.
Very close, yes.
Very close.
In school, I hardly ever felt-- I mean, there would be some kids called you a Jew or something.
But not openly, like anybody to do any harm, or to persecute us, no, no.
Yes, very close, yeah.
Well, I used to play more with my older brother Yakov.
He was only two years older.
So I used to play with him and do some work around the house.
I liked to do always something to fix around the house and so on.
And he would be helping me to do some fixing around the house.
Oh, yeah.
I always-- I liked always to do something, not to stand still, just always to build something or repair.
Fairly good, yes.
Well, later on, when I went to a mechanical school-- actually, mechanics were always my favorite studies.
But as a boy, nature.
I liked nature always, farming and studying about farm.
And since we lived in a village, we did a lot of planting in the school and during the harvest, so on-- before the harvest, studying about crops and everything else.
And I liked it.
But mainly, my-- I was good in poetry, for some reason, and singing.
Yes, like any other [LAUGHS] Jewish-- Eastern Jewish families, which would be chicken soup, and kugel, and gefilte fish, and all these [NON-ENGLISH] which were made in those days.
